title: Corgi, the buzzy Y Combinator-backed insurance tech startup, says it didn’t steal an open source product
summary: Corgi became embroiled in controversy when Papermark accused it of stealing its software. Corgi says it did not, raising new questions about vibe coding.
whatHappening: Corgi, a Y Combinator-backed insurance tech startup, is denying allegations that it stole open source software from Papermark. The dispute has ignited debate about the practice of 'vibe coding' in the startup world.
whoInvolved: Corgi (Y Combinator-backed insurance startup) and Papermark (open source software creator) are at the center of the dispute.
whyMatters: The accusation raises serious questions about intellectual property practices in tech startups and whether founders are appropriately crediting or licensing open source code. This incident highlights the tension between rapid startup development and ethical software development practices.

title: Novak Djokovic has a new job —  advisor to private equity firm General Atlantic
summary: General Atlantic has tapped tennis legend Novak Djokovic to serve as a global strategic advisor.
whatHappening: Novak Djokovic is joining General Atlantic as a global strategic advisor. The tennis champion is expanding beyond sports into the private equity world.
whoInvolved: Novak Djokovic, the tennis legend, and General Atlantic, a major private equity firm.
whyMatters: The move signals Djokovic's pivot toward business and investment after his dominant tennis career. It also represents General Atlantic's strategy to leverage celebrity influence and networks for deal-making and portfolio guidance.

title: Trump Admin releases Anthropic  Mythos to be used by more than 100 US companies, agencies
summary: Over 100 companies and government agencies are reportedly authorized to use Mythos 5, including their non-American employees.
whatHappening: The Trump administration is releasing Anthropic's Mythos 5 AI model for use by over 100 U.S. companies and government agencies. The authorization extends access to both American and non-American employees within these organizations.
whoInvolved: Anthropic (AI company), the Trump administration, and 100+ U.S. companies and government agencies.
whyMatters: This marks a significant expansion of advanced AI access across the private and public sectors, potentially accelerating AI adoption in critical industries and government functions. The decision to include non-American employees suggests a shift in how the administration is managing AI technology access policies.

title: FTC gives Musk the OK to acquire SpaceX alumni startup Mesh
summary: Mesh came out of stealth in February with a $50 million Series A.
whatHappening: The FTC has approved Elon Musk's acquisition of Mesh, a startup founded by former SpaceX employees. Mesh emerged from stealth in February with $50 million in Series A funding.
whoInvolved: Elon Musk, Mesh (SpaceX alumni startup), and the Federal Trade Commission.
whyMatters: The approval clears the way for Musk to expand his portfolio with talent and technology from former SpaceX team members. This acquisition could strengthen capabilities across Musk's companies, though regulatory scrutiny suggests concentration of resources was a consideration.

title: The fittest founder in the room got cancer. Here’s how he used AI to fight back.
summary: When confronted with cancer, Connor Christou fed everything tied tied to his regime — blood results, scan data, wearable output, journal entries — into Claude.
whatHappening: A tech founder diagnosed with cancer is using Claude AI to analyze his personal health data—blood results, scans, wearables, and journal entries—to inform his treatment strategy. He's leveraging AI as a tool to fight back against the disease.
whoInvolved: Connor Christou, a founder known for fitness, working with Claude (Anthropic's AI model).
whyMatters: This illustrates how AI is moving from theoretical applications into deeply personal healthcare decisions, showing both the potential and real-world stakes of AI in medicine. It raises questions about AI's role in patient empowerment versus the need for medical expertise.

title: Asian AI startups launch Mythos-like  models as Anthropic’s export ban drags on
summary: New models are launching in Asia that promise Mythos-like capabilities without fear of an export ban. U.S. AI labs may never recover this enormous market.
whatHappening: Asian AI startups are launching models with capabilities comparable to Anthropic's offerings, capitalizing on uncertainty around U.S. export restrictions. These new models aim to fill the market gap created by regulatory concerns.
whoInvolved: Asian AI startups, Anthropic, and U.S. AI labs.
whyMatters: The export ban risk is driving talent and market share away from American AI companies to international competitors. U.S. AI labs risk losing dominance in the massive Asian market to homegrown alternatives.

title: 24 Prime Day deals Verge readers are grabbing before Prime Day ends
summary: The tiny Xteink X4 reader is proving surprisingly popular. There are an overwhelming number of Prime Day deals to sort through, which is why we spend so much time highlighting products we’ve already tested and can stand behind. But our recommendations are only one way to…
whatHappening: The Verge is highlighting 24 Prime Day deals that readers are actively purchasing before the sale ends. The Xteink X4 reader is emerging as a surprising customer favorite among the available offers.
whoInvolved: The Verge editorial team and Amazon Prime Day shoppers are the primary parties involved.
whyMatters: With Prime Day's overwhelming deal volume, curated recommendations from trusted reviewers help consumers make informed purchasing decisions. This article cuts through the noise by featuring only products The Verge has tested and endorsed, saving readers time and money during the sales event.

title: Apple Vision Pro exec is reportedly leaving for OpenAI
summary: Paul Meade, the Apple vice president in charge of the Vision Pro headset, is reportedly leaving the company to join OpenAI’s hardware team.
whatHappening: Paul Meade, Apple's VP overseeing the Vision Pro headset, is departing the company. He's joining OpenAI's hardware division.
whoInvolved: Paul Meade (Apple VP), Apple, and OpenAI.
whyMatters: The exit signals potential shifts in Apple's Vision Pro strategy while strengthening OpenAI's hardware ambitions. It reflects talent movement between two of tech's most prominent companies in the race for next-generation computing platforms.

title: This might be the new best smart speaker
summary: Hi, friends! Welcome to Installer No. 134, your guide to the best and Verge-iest stuff in the world. (If you're new here, welcome, hope you're okay in all this heat, and also you can read all the old editions at the Installer homepage.) This week, I've been reading about…
whatHappening: The Verge's Installer newsletter is highlighting what might be the new best smart speaker on the market. The publication is featuring this week's tech recommendations and product reviews.
whoInvolved: The Verge's editorial team is curating and reviewing smart speaker products for their audience.
whyMatters: Smart speakers are increasingly central to home automation and daily routines, making reliable product recommendations important for consumers evaluating which device to buy. The Verge's endorsement can significantly influence purchasing decisions in this competitive market segment.

title: It’s the last day of Prime Day — here are over 140 great deals to choose from
summary: We’ve arrived at the final day of Prime Day, which at this point should probably be called “Prime Week.” We’ve found discounts on all manner of gadgets, including TVs, smart home tech, chargers, headphones, and more. Some of the best deals have started selling out at some…
whatHappening: Amazon Prime Day is ending today with over 140 active deals across gadgets, TVs, smart home devices, and accessories. Some of the best discounts are already selling out as shoppers rush to grab final bargains.
whoInvolved: Amazon is running the sale; The Verge is curating and covering the deals.
whyMatters: This is a key shopping event where consumers can save significantly on tech purchases before inventory runs dry. The rapid sellouts signal strong consumer demand and shrinking windows for deal-hunters to act.

title: Indie developers got tired of waiting for a new Star Fox, so they’re making their own
summary: Nostalgia remains a powerful force. So much so that, in exploring the echoes of a late-'90s childhood spent skimming the water of Corneria and sneering "cocky little freaks!" in time with a monkey encased in a Gundam suit, I'm simultaneously describing playing Star Fox 64 (Lylat…
whatHappening: Indie developers are creating their own Star Fox game because Nintendo hasn't released a new entry in the beloved franchise in years. Fans are channeling nostalgia for the classic late-'90s space shooter into homemade alternatives.
whoInvolved: Independent game developers and Star Fox fans frustrated with Nintendo's lack of new releases in the series.
whyMatters: This reflects how beloved gaming franchises can languish without new official entries, creating an opening for passionate fan communities to fill the gap. It highlights both the enduring appeal of classic games and indie developers' ability to meet demand when major publishers don't.

title: Why is Apple asking me to pay more for Big Tech’s AI obsession?
summary: Tim Cook recently said price increases were "unavoidable" and described the company's pricing as "unsustainable." The 16-inch MacBook Pro saw its price go up by $300. The 11-inch iPad Air went from $599 to $749. Even the HomePod Mini got a $30 bump to $129. Cook squarely placed…
whatHappening: Apple is raising prices across its product lineup, including a $300 increase on the 16-inch MacBook Pro and hikes on the iPad Air and HomePod Mini. CEO Tim Cook is framing these increases as unavoidable and necessary.
whoInvolved: Apple and CEO Tim Cook are driving the price increases across multiple product categories.
whyMatters: Consumers are being asked to pay more for devices as tech companies invest heavily in AI capabilities and infrastructure. This reflects broader industry pressure to fund the AI arms race, potentially pricing out budget-conscious buyers and raising questions about whether these upgrades justify the cost.

title: The Guardian’s Kai Wright refuses to buy a new phone
summary: That is an objectively dope couch. | Image: Kai Wright Kai Wright is the co-host of Stateside with Kai and Carter over at the Guardian. But Wright has been bringing his unique insights to listeners for years. He's also hosted Notes From America, The United States of Anxiety, and…
whatHappening: Kai Wright, co-host of Stateside with Kai and Carter at The Guardian, is refusing to buy a new phone. The decision highlights a stance against unnecessary consumer electronics upgrades.
whoInvolved: Kai Wright, a long-time audio journalist and host at The Guardian who has also produced shows including Notes From America and The United States of Anxiety.
whyMatters: Wright's choice reflects growing consumer resistance to constant tech upgrades and planned obsolescence in the smartphone industry. It raises questions about sustainability, consumerism, and the durability of modern devices.

title: Inside the room where the smart home industry is still betting on Matter
summary: Four years ago, overlooking a canal in Amsterdam, the smart home industry collectively launched Matter, the one interoperability standard to rule them all. Heralded as the solution to the industry's struggles, Matter was built on open standards and existing technologies and is…
whatHappening: The smart home industry is still doubling down on Matter, the interoperability standard launched four years ago in Amsterdam. Despite early promises, the industry continues betting on this open-standard solution to solve fragmentation problems.
whoInvolved: Multiple smart home companies and industry players are backing Matter as the unified standard for device compatibility.
whyMatters: Matter represents a critical attempt to break down silos in the smart home market where competing platforms have historically locked users into proprietary ecosystems. If successful, it could finally enable seamless device interoperability across brands; if it fails, consumers remain trapped in fragmented product ecosystems.

title: This puzzle game’s simple premise hides surprising depth
summary: What's the Password? has a simple concept: To solve each of the game's more than 100 puzzles, you have to type in the right four-digit password on a number pad. That might sound like a limited constraint. But the simplicity gives solo developer Dan DiIorio, better known as…
whatHappening: A new puzzle game called What's the Password? is gaining attention for its deceptively simple design. Players solve over 100 puzzles by entering four-digit passwords on a number pad.
whoInvolved: Solo developer Dan DiIorio created the game, and The Verge is covering it.
whyMatters: The game demonstrates how severe constraints can foster creativity and depth rather than limit it. It showcases the potential for minimalist design to deliver engaging gameplay experiences.

title: Prime Day is almost over, but these are still the best Apple deals I’ve seen
summary: Amazon’s Prime Day is now in its final hours, but whether you’re looking for a new pair of wireless earbuds or a smartwatch, there’s a good chance you’ll still find a discount. The Apple Watch Series 11 remains at its best price to date, while the AirPods Pro 3 are still on sale…
whatHappening: Amazon's Prime Day is in its final hours with steep discounts still available on Apple products. The Apple Watch Series 11 and AirPods Pro 3 are among the devices hitting their lowest prices.
whoInvolved: Amazon, Apple, and consumers shopping for tech deals.
whyMatters: This is the last chance for shoppers to grab premium Apple gear at significant discounts before Prime Day ends. These deals represent rare opportunities to save on typically expensive products.

title: Prime Day’s final hours bring rare discounts on Philips Hue smart lights
summary: Philips Hue products don’t often see major discounts, which makes this year’s Prime Day deals especially notable. Prices have dropped significantly across much of the company’s smart lighting lineup, with deals on everything from smart bulb starter kits and sleep lamps to smart…
whatHappening: Philips Hue smart lights are experiencing rare, significant price cuts during Prime Day's final hours. Discounts are spanning the company's entire product lineup, including starter kits and specialty items.
whoInvolved: Amazon (running Prime Day) and Philips Hue are the main players in these deals.
whyMatters: Philips Hue products rarely go on sale, making this Prime Day event a genuine opportunity for consumers to purchase premium smart lighting at lower prices. This could drive adoption of the brand among price-conscious buyers who typically avoid its premium pricing.

title: The 17 best robot vacuum deals you can still get before Prime Day ends
summary: Our former top pick the Roborock S8 MaxV Ultra is now just $600, which is more than half its original price at launch. | Image: Roborock If you’ve been wanting to buy a robot vacuum but have been put off by how much it can cost to get a good one, now is not a bad time to start…
whatHappening: Robot vacuum prices are dropping dramatically during Prime Day, with top-tier models like the Roborock S8 MaxV Ultra now selling for over 50% off their original prices. The deals are still available but won't last long as the event winds down.
whoInvolved: Roborock (manufacturer of premium robot vacuums) and Amazon (Prime Day retailer) are the main players, with The Verge reporting on the deals.
whyMatters: This makes high-quality robot vacuums accessible to budget-conscious consumers who've previously avoided the category due to high costs, potentially expanding the market for smart home cleaning devices.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos 5 is back
summary: After a rollercoaster negotiation process with the Trump administration that dragged on for two weeks, Anthropic's Mythos 5 is finally back in action - at least, somewhat, for a select group of organizations, according to a letter from the government to Anthropic that was viewed…
whatHappening: Anthropic's Mythos 5 is resuming operations after a two-week negotiation with the Trump administration. The AI model is being reactivated for a limited group of organizations.
whoInvolved: Anthropic, the Trump administration, and select organizations with access to Mythos 5.
whyMatters: This signals a potential resolution to regulatory tensions around advanced AI development and deployment. The selective rollout suggests ongoing government oversight of how powerful AI systems are distributed and used.
